articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Rev. William Douglass of St. Thomas
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar (Pencil Pusher)
articles.summary: Retrospective (Phila. Tribune, 1916) on Rev. William Douglass, born Sept. 6, 1805 in Baltimore, pupil of Daniel Coker, who came to Philadelphia and in 1843 became rector of St. Thomas' African Episcopal Church (vacant in effect since founder Absalom Jones died 1818). Recruited through a conversation among Rev. John F. Cook, James Needham and Robert Gordon; his wife was a daughter of Hezekiah Grice. Describes the 'renaissance period' community that greeted him: James Forten, Robert Bogle, Bishop Richard Allen, Robert Douglass, John Bowers, Bishop Morris Brown, Junius C. Morel, Robert Douglass Jr., William Whipper, Robert Purvis, John C. Bowers, Frederick Hinton, Stephen Gloucester, Charles Gardner, and the Library Company of Colored Persons in 'full blast.'

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Liberty Hall and the Institute for Colored Youth
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar (Pencil Pusher)
articles.summary: (Nov. 16, 1912) History of the building at 716-718 Lombard St.: home of the Institute for Colored Youth 1854-1866 (teachers Charles L. Reason, E. D. Bassett, Sarah M. Douglass, Robert Campbell, Grace A. Mapps, Jacob C. White Jr., Martha A. Farbeaux), then bought in 1866 by a band of public-spirited men and remodeled as Liberty Hall. Owners included William Whipper, William Still, Rev. Stephen Smith, William Warrick, Henry Jones, Bishop J. J. Clinton, Rev. Elisha Weaver, Robert Adger and James W. Purnell. Scene of Fifteenth Amendment meetings (Richard T. Greener's role noted), Banneker Institute and Pythian Club rooms, lectures by Rev. Dr. Blyden, appearances by Frederick Douglass, Frances E. W. Harper, and singers Thomas J. Bowers and Elizabeth Taylor Greenfield ('The Black Swan').

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Denmark Vesey, Morris Brown, and the Francis affair
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar (Pencil Pusher)
articles.summary: (Feb. 15, 1913) Links Charleston to Philadelphia: after the 1822 Denmark Vesey uprising, Morris Brown Sr. — suspected because he led Charleston's Allenite Methodists — fled to Philadelphia, joined Bishop Richard Allen, and became the A.M.E. Church's second bishop. Also recounts a c.1859 incident: a Charleston slaveholder named Francis, staying at William Still's boarding house at 832 South St., spotted a fugitive returned from Canada; Mrs. Carr and Robert Adger spirited the man to Mrs. Miller's (mother of Robert Purvis) and on to Canada, while young men of the Phoenix Club (922 South St.) — Joseph and Edward Y. Venning, William H. Johnson, Robert M. Adger, Charles Bundy, Winter C. Morton, Corbyn and Silas Taylor, George Boardley, Samuel Morgan Smith — plotted to tar and feather Francis before Adger and Still got him away safely.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — The Emancipation semi-centennial and the record of exhibitions
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar (Pencil Pusher)
articles.summary: (Jan. 27, 1912) On Pennsylvania's $20,000 commission (with Harry W. Bass, first Black member of the legislature) to commemorate the Emancipation semi-centennial. Recalls precedents: the 1838 survey of Black Philadelphia's industrial, educational, religious and material condition conceived by the Quakers and assisted by the colored people themselves; an 1851 handicrafts exhibition at the Philadelphia Institute; a women's curio exhibition c.1887 aided by William H. Dorsey and Robert M. Adger; and the American Negro Historical Society's collecting spirit (Adger, Jacob C. White, Dorsey).

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — The Fifteenth Amendment celebration
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar (Pencil Pusher)
articles.summary: (Apr. 19, 1913) Retrospective of Philadelphia's great celebration of the Fifteenth Amendment (April 1870) — bigger than Harrisburg's or Pittsburgh's, funded almost entirely by colored people. Joseph C. Bustill was promoter and general manager; Andrew J. Jones secretary. Ward committees included Thomas J. Davis, James A. Junior, David Knight, Isaiah C. Wear, Jacob C. White, David B. Bowser, George Hall, O. V. Catto, Silas Taylor, John C. Bowers, Thomas J. Bowers, William Whipper, Henry Jones, Thomas J. Dorsey, Rev. Stephen Smith, John B. Reeve, Andrew F. Stevens, Jacob C. White Jr., Thomas Charnock, James F. Needham, J. W. Purnell, Warley Bascom, Raymond J. Burr, the Vennings, Robert and Robert M. Adger, and others. Main celebration at Horticultural Hall with orators Robert Purvis, Isaiah C. Wear, William Howard Day and Octavius V. Catto. Notes some men present had been Pennsylvania voters before 1838 — voting again for the first time since disenfranchisement.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — First organs in the colored churches
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar (Pencil Pusher)
articles.summary: (Jun. 20, 1914) History of church music: the first pipe organ in any colored church of Philadelphia was installed at St. Thomas' in the 1830s, built by J. C. Stanbridge, first played by Miss Ann Appo (sister of musicians William and Joseph Appo, who played in the Walnut Street Theatre orchestra in 1826). John C. Bowers was the second organist, playing one instrument forty years. Henry Williams, composer, was first player at First African Presbyterian; Elizabeth Taylor Greenfield ('the Black Swan') played Shiloh's first organ. Rev. Charles Gardner instituted sacred song recitals at First African Presbyterian nearly 75 years ago with members of Frank Johnson's band (E. D. Roland leader), leading to a performance of Haydn's 'Creation' in 1840-41 with a chorus of 150. Joseph Carter, a colored mechanical genius, built pipe organs for Crucifixion, Shiloh, Central and Union Baptist; Robert M. Adger apprenticed under him.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Old race newspapers: The Echo and its era
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar (Pencil Pusher)
articles.summary: (May 2, 1914) Survey of Philadelphia's Black newspapers of the 1880s-90s: The Echo (1882, L. J. Coppin and William T. Simpson editors, Abel P. Caldwell business manager), The Sun (1891, G. W. Gardiner), The Standard Echo (1895), The Sunday Journal (Robert G. Still), The Sunday Herald (1896, Thomas Wallace Swann). Notes Smedley's 'Underground Railroad' history was finished after his death by Robert Purvis and Marianna Gibbons.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — George Thompson, anti-slavery immortal
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar (Pencil Pusher)
articles.summary: (Nov. 23, 1912) On English abolitionist George Thompson's American visit (c.1834-35), drawn by Garrison, Lundy and Mott after the first conventions held in Philadelphia 1831-36. Southern papers put a price on his head. In Philadelphia he was entertained socially by James Forten, while the young men of that day — John C. Bowers, James McCrummell, Robert Purvis, Frederick Hinton, Junius C. Morel, James Needham, William Whipper and James C. Matthews — organized meetings and social amenities. He visited the churches and schools of free Black Philadelphia and publicized their achievements in Britain, where Dr. James McCune Smith of New York and fugitive Moses Roper assisted at his meetings. His picture survives in the Adger collection at the Home for Aged and Infirm Colored Persons.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Four more race newspapers
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar (Pencil Pusher)
articles.summary: (May 9, 1914) Continued newspaper survey: The Sun (1892, George W. Gardiner, Rev. William H. Heard associate), The Standard Echo (Apr. 1895, Abel P. Caldwell) with its account of a Frederick Douglass Monumental Association and the Caterers Supply Co. (P. A. Dutrieuille, Walter P. Hall, John S. Trower and others), and Robert G. Still's Sunday Journal with the roster of the Citizens Republican Club (Andrew F. Stevens, president). Notes Robert Purvis was then the only man of his race in the Philadelphia Blue Book.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Relics of early Methodism and the General Conference of 1848
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar (Pencil Pusher)
articles.summary: (Oct. 19, 1912) Urges collecting relics of A.M.E. history before the 1916 centennial — citing Robert M. Adger's discovery of an early portrait of Bishop Richard Allen and the collections at the Home for Aged and Infirm Colored Persons and the American Negro Historical Society. Then details the Eighth General Conference of the A.M.E. Church, Philadelphia, May 1, 1848: Bishop William Paul Quinn presiding, M. M. Clark secretary, A. W. Wayman and A. Davis assistant secretaries, 74 delegates from nine states and Canada. Prominent figures: Daniel A. Payne ('omnipresent'), George Hogarth, John Peck, N. C. W. Cannon, Rev. Stephen Smith, Dr. J. J. G. Bias, Willis Nazrey, David Ware, John Cornish, Peter Gardner, John M. Brown (later bishop), A. R. Green, Walter Proctor, Joshua Woodlyn and others.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Pioneer Black newspapers, from Freedom's Journal to the Daily Advocate
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar (Pencil Pusher)
articles.summary: (Apr. 1914) History of the race press: John B. Russwurm's Freedom's Journal (New York, 1827) and Rights of All; Benjamin Stanley's Demosthenian Shield (Philadelphia, 1840); and Philadelphia's claim to the first magazine — The Moral Reformer, edited by William Whipper in 1837. Also the Anglo-African (1859; Parker T. Smith special writer, Jacob C. White Jr. Philadelphia business representative), Centennial-era Black journalists on white dailies, T. Morris Chester's Civil War correspondence for The Press, and the Daily Advocate (Feb. 1877, S. L. Nichols and Henry H. Griffin), whose advertisers included Thomas J. Bowers (coal), Robert M. Adger (furniture under Liberty Hall), and A. P. Ashbourne (cocoanut manufacturer).

events.name: George Thompson's visit to Philadelphia's Black community
events.description: English abolitionist George Thompson, hounded by pro-slavery mobs and papers, was entertained socially by James Forten; the young men of the community organized meetings and social amenities for him (recalled in Pencil Pusher Points, Nov. 23, 1912).

events.name: Eighth General Conference of the A.M.E. Church
events.description: 74 delegates from PA, NJ, DE, NY, the Eastern States, MD, OH, IN and Canada; ministers of other denominations invited to seats without vote (recalled in Pencil Pusher Points, Oct. 19, 1912).

events.name: Fifteenth Amendment celebration, Philadelphia
events.description: Grand celebration of ratification of the 15th Amendment, organized and funded by the colored people; some participants had been Pennsylvania voters before the 1838 disenfranchisement (recalled in Pencil Pusher Points, Apr. 19, 1913).

events.name: Purchase and founding of Liberty Hall
events.description: A band of public-spirited men purchased the old Institute for Colored Youth building and remodeled it as Liberty Hall (recalled in Pencil Pusher Points, Nov. 16, 1912).

events.name: Performance of Haydn's 'Creation'
events.description: Sacred-music landmark: 'The Creation' sung in 1840-41 with principals, a chorus of 150 and a large orchestra, growing out of Rev. Charles Gardner's sacred song recitals with members of Frank Johnson's band, E. D. Roland leader (recalled in Pencil Pusher Points, Jun. 20, 1914).
